translatedText: The Anglo-Spanish War broke out
Francis Drake raided Cadiz, heavily defeating the Spanish fleet
February 8, 1587
Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded
Spain formed the Spanish Armada
England sent a massive expeditionary fleet (the Drake-Norris Expedition) against Spain
Dispatched reinforcements to support Henry IV of France's succession to the throne
The English expeditionary force sent to France was annihilated
The war against the Irish rebels led by Hugh O'Neill began
Spring 1599
The English army commanded by Robert Devereux was defeated in Ireland
December 31, 1600
Granted a royal charter to the English East India Company
The English House of Commons petitioned to abolish the abuses of price-fixing monopolies
February 25, 1601
Robert Devereux, Earl of Essex, was executed
February 1603
Her health rapidly declined following the deaths of her close friends
March 24, 1603
Died at Richmond Palace
Long Reign and the Last Monarch of the Tudor Dynasty
Ascending to the throne in 1558, she reigned as the monarch of the Kingdoms of England and Ireland for 44 years until her death in 1603, becoming the final ruler of the Tudor dynasty.
Settlement of the Church of England
She reversed Mary I's religious policies and reasserted separation from the Catholic Church, firmly establishing the Church of England.
Symbolism as the Virgin Queen
Remaining steadfast in her celibacy despite numerous proposals, she was known as the 'Virgin Queen', and the U.S. state of Virginia was named in her honor.
Collaboration with Competent Officials
She managed state affairs by cooperating with Parliament and utilizing the assistance of capable officials such as William Cecil.
Execution of Mary, Queen of Scots
Utilizing the intelligence network led by Francis Walsingham, she thwarted the plots of Mary, Queen of Scots, and had her executed to eliminate the threat to her throne.
Defeat of the Spanish Armada
She played a key role in the English Navy's crushing defeat of the Spanish Armada during the Anglo-Spanish War.
Chartering of the English East India Company
On December 31, 1600, she chartered the English East India Company, laying the groundwork for overseas colonial expansion.
Whose daughter was Elizabeth I?
She was born to Henry VIII and his second wife, Anne Boleyn.
Why was Elizabeth I called the 'Virgin Queen'?
She came to be known as the 'Virgin Queen' by insisting on remaining single despite numerous marriage proposals, and in 1599, she declared that she was 'married to the nation'.
What were Elizabeth I's religious policies?
She reversed Mary I's pro-Catholic restoration policies and firmly established the Church of England, adopting a tolerant attitude towards Catholicism while strictly regulating Puritans.
What was the relationship between Mary, Queen of Scots, and Elizabeth I?
Mary, Queen of Scots, posed a potential threat by asserting her claim to the English throne and was eventually beheaded by Elizabeth I following the Babington Plot.
What was the most important foreign affair during Elizabeth I's reign?
The defeat of the Spanish Armada during the Anglo-Spanish War was the most significant, alongside overseas colonial expansion and the chartering of the English East India Company.
Born in Ireland on February 15
Joined Robert Falcon Scott's Antarctic expedition
Finally selected as a member of the National Antarctic Expedition in March
The Discovery set sail for Antarctica on August 6
Discovered a massive iceberg on January 2
Escaped the pack ice on January 8
Landed at Granite Harbour in South Victoria Land on January 20 and collected rock samples
Discovered new land on January 30
Took the first aerial photographs of Antarctica from a hot air balloon at an altitude of 240m on February 4
Anchored near McMurdo Sound on February 8
Discovered the first overland route leading to the South Pole between February 19 and 22
Scott, Wilson, and Shackleton departed for the South Pole on November 2
Declared the Antarctic expedition a failure at 81 degrees south latitude on December 19
Placed on a sled and headed back to base on January 30
Returned to the Discovery on February 3
Boarded a relief ship and returned to London on March 1
Arrived in England on June 12
Personally led an Antarctic expedition
Completed fundraising on March 25
Departed from England on August 7
Appointed a Member of the Royal Victorian Order (MVO, 4th class)
Edgeworth David, a member of the expedition, became the first to summit Mount Erebus
Reached a record furthest south latitude of 88° 23' on January 9
Returned to England in June
Appointed a Commander of the Royal Victorian Order (CVO, 3rd class)
Knighted
Continued Antarctic exploration
Resumed the voyage of the Endurance from South Georgia on December 5
Encountered the first iceberg on December 8
Navigated through the pack ice on December 11
Arrived at Caird Coast (Coats Land) on January 12
Broke free from the pack ice and headed for the open sea on October 15
The Endurance was crushed in the pack ice and wrecked on October 27
The pack ice melted after 70 days on November 7
Left Ocean Camp on December 23
Launched boats and headed for land, arriving at Elephant Island on April 10
A six-man advance party set sail for South Georgia to seek rescue on April 24
Arrived on the southern coast of South Georgia on May 8
Shackleton, Worsley, and Crean began crossing South Georgia on May 15
Arrived at the whaling station on May 20
Successfully rescued all remaining crew members stranded on Elephant Island on October 8
Awarded an Officer of the Order of the British Empire (OBE, Military Division)
Continued Antarctic exploration
The Quest, carrying Shackleton, set sail for South Georgia in September
Landed at Stromness Bay in South Georgia on January 4
Died of a fatal heart attack caused by atheroma on January 5
First Ascent of Mount Erebus (Expedition Team)
In 1908, Edgeworth David, a member of the expedition he commanded, became the first person in the world to summit Mount Erebus.
Reached 88° 23' South Latitude
During the Nimrod Expedition in 1909, he reached a record furthest south latitude of 88° 23'.
Rescue of the Endurance Crew
During the Endurance Expedition in 1916, amidst extreme conditions in Antarctica, he rescued all 27 crew members without a single casualty.
Taking Photographs of Antarctica
